part of the Imaging Hackathon in Freiburg. This is a follow-up PR of #17556.

## Contributions

This PR adds content assertion XML tags for automated test output verification using 2-D single-channel and multi-channel images. The contributed XML tags are detailed below.

### `image_has_metadata`

The `image_has_metadata` XML tag can be used for verification of the **metadata** of the images generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Currently supported metadata is:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `width` | The width of the image. |
| `height` | The height of the image. |
| `channels` | Number of channels. |

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_metadata width="512" height="512" channels="1" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

### `image_has_intensities`

The `image_has_intensities` XML tag can be used for verification of the **image intensities** of the images generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Verification of the following features is supported:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `mean_intensity` | The mean image intensity. |
| `mean_intensity_min` | Lower bound of the mean image intensity. |
| `mean_intensity_max` | Upper bound of the mean image intensity. |
| `center_of_mass` | The center of mass of the image intensities. |
| `eps` | Absolute tolerance for `mean_intensity` and `center_of_mass` assertions. |
| `channel` | Restricts the assertions to only a specific image channel. |

The assertions are imposed for all image channels if the `channel` attribute is omitted.

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_intensities channel="0" mean_intensity="0.24" eps="0.01" />
      <image_has_intensities channel="1" mean_intensity="0.66" eps="0.01" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

### `image_has_labels`

The `image_has_labels` XML tag can be used for verification of **label images (label maps)** generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Verification of the following features is supported:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `number_of_objects` | The number of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size` | The mean size (number of pixels) of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size_min` | Lower bound of the mean size of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size_max` | Upper bound of the mean size of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `exclude_labels` | Excludes labels from the other assertions. |
| `eps` | Absolute tolerance for the `mean_object_size` assertion. |

The primary use case of the `exclude_labels` attribute is to exclude the image background from the `number_of_objects` and `mean_object_size` assertions, which is often labeled by `0` or `-1`.

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_labels number_of_objects="187" exclude_labels="0" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

part of the Imaging Hackathon in Freiburg. This is a follow-up PR of #17556.

## Contributions

This PR adds content assertion XML tags for automated test output verification using 2-D single-channel and multi-channel images. The contributed XML tags are detailed below.

### `image_has_metadata`

The `image_has_metadata` XML tag can be used for verification of the **metadata** of the images generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Currently supported metadata is:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `width` | The width of the image. |
| `height` | The height of the image. |
| `channels` | Number of channels. |

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_metadata width="512" height="512" channels="1" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

### `image_has_intensities`

The `image_has_intensities` XML tag can be used for verification of the **image intensities** of the images generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Verification of the following features is supported:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `mean_intensity` | The mean image intensity. |
| `mean_intensity_min` | Lower bound of the mean image intensity. |
| `mean_intensity_max` | Upper bound of the mean image intensity. |
| `center_of_mass` | The center of mass of the image intensities. |
| `eps` | Absolute tolerance for `mean_intensity` and `center_of_mass` assertions. |
| `channel` | Restricts the assertions to only a specific image channel. |

The assertions are imposed for all image channels if the `channel` attribute is omitted.

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_intensities channel="0" mean_intensity="0.24" eps="0.01" />
      <image_has_intensities channel="1" mean_intensity="0.66" eps="0.01" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

### `image_has_labels`

The `image_has_labels` XML tag can be used for verification of **label images (label maps)** generated by a Galaxy tool wrapper. Verification of the following features is supported:

| Attribute | Feature description |
| --- | --- |
| `number_of_objects` | The number of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size` | The mean size (number of pixels) of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size_min` | Lower bound of the mean size of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `mean_object_size_max` | Upper bound of the mean size of uniquely labeled objects. |
| `exclude_labels` | Excludes labels from the other assertions. |
| `eps` | Absolute tolerance for the `mean_object_size` assertion. |

The primary use case of the `exclude_labels` attribute is to exclude the image background from the `number_of_objects` and `mean_object_size` assertions, which is often labeled by `0` or `-1`.

Example:
```xml
<test>
  <output name="output">
    <assert_contents>
      <image_has_labels number_of_objects="187" exclude_labels="0" />
    </assert_contents>
  </output>
</test>
```

{"action":"opened","number":17866,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/galaxyproject/galaxy/pulls/17866","id":1797784576,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAdw-Nc5rKAQA","html_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17866","diff_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17866.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17866.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/17866","number":17866,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add `pin_labels` attribute for `image_diff` comparison method","user":{"login":"kostrykin","id":6557139,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NTcxMzk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6557139?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin","html_url":"https://github.com/kostrykin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kostrykin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556 we had introduced the `image_diff` comparison method for output verification, which supports different metrics for quantification of the image differences.

The `iou` metric is designated for verification of label maps (images where the values correspond to labels of image regions). Usually the particular label values are interchangeable and only required to be distinct in order to be able to distinguish the differently labeled image regions. This fact is taken into account by the `iou` metric, which performs a pairwise comparison of uniquely labeled image regions, where pairs of corresponding regions are determined by maximizing the IoU.

However, there also often are cases where specific label values are *not* interchangeable. The most prominent example is a segmentation mask, where the image background is canonically labeled by 0 or -1, but the label values of the individual objects still are interchangeable. To also take such use cases into account, this PR adds the `pin_labels` attribute:

```xml
<output name="out" value="res.tiff" compare="image_diff" metric="iou" pin_labels="0" />
```

The `pin_labels` attribute can be used to specify one or more labels, which are not interchangeable. For image regions labeled with those label values, the correspondences are established not by maximization of the IoU, but according to the label value itself.

Current Behavior `conda env create -f environment.yml` gets stuck if pip dependencies are installed and pip gives a user prompt. ### Steps to Reproduce Let's create an environment with `conda env create -f environment.yml` and install a package with pip: ```name: channels: - conda-forge - defaults dependencies: - pip - pip: - "--editable=git+ssh:///" ``` However, we previously installed that package through pip from a different repository: `pip install --editable=git+ssh:///` In this case, pip will prompt the user for input: ``` (base) [user@vm directory]$ pip install --editable=git+ssh:/// Obtaining from git+ssh:/// WARNING: git clone in ./src/ exists with URL ssh:/// WARNING: The plan is to install the git repository ssh:/// What to do? (s)witch, (i)gnore, (w)ipe, (b)ackup ``` If you run `conda env create -f environment.yml`, conda will get to the `Installing pip dependencies` step and get stuck there without feedback as pip is waiting on a response to the prompt, but conda can't show the prompt to the user until pip is done installing the dependencies. ## Expected Behavior Tough. I guess there is no easy/good way to avoid this, but it would be nice if either: - pip stdout is streamed in realtime to the console - conda notices the user prompt and either gives a warning, or passes the prompt on to the user - a web search returns this issue as a result if you search for 'conda stuck on installing pip dependencies' so you at least know where to look. Then you can try to run the pip install manually and solve the issues before running `conda env create` - ??? ## Environment Information

:"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method for test output verification using images.

Previous comparison methods comprise `diff`, `re_match`, `lines_diff`, `re_match_multiline`, `contains`, and `sim_size`, all of which are either unsuitable for image data, or dangerous to be used (because the size of a file does not really represent its content). This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method, which is specifically tailored for single-channel and multi-channel image data (e.g., RGB), and can be used like this:

```xml
<test>
    <param name="in" value="actual.png" />
    <output name="out" value="expected.tif" compare="image_diff" metric="mae" />
</test>
```

The difference of a pair of images is quantified as the pixel-wise distance between the images, for which different *metrics* can be used. For different applications and different types of images (e.g., intensity vs. label images), different types of metrics are common. The following metrics are added for *intensity images:*

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for intensity images) |
| --- | --- |
| `mae` | Mean absolute error |
| `mse` | Mean squared error |
| `rms` | Root mean squared error |
| `fro` | Frobenius norm |

For label images (label maps), it is usually irrelevant which particular label is used for labeling an image region, but only the *sets of pixels* corresponding to individual image regions need to be compared. This can be accomplished using *one minus* the *intersection of the union* of the regions:

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for label images) |
| --- | --- |
| `iou` | One minus the intersection of the union |

To decide whether a pair of images is equal, a tolerance is required, because different compression algorithms (or different versions thereof) can lead to different compression artifacts. A pair of images is considered to be *equal* in terms of the specified metric, if the distance of the images computed with respect to the metric is not above a threshold `eps` (0.01 by default).

## How to test the changes?
(Select all options that apply)
- [x] I've included appropriate [automated tests](https://docs.galaxyproject.org/en/latest/dev/writing_tests.html).
- [ ] This is a refactoring of components with existing test coverage.
- [ ] Instructions for manual testing are as follows:
  1. [add testing steps and prerequisites here if you didn't write automated tests covering all your changes]

This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method for test output verification using images.

Previous comparison methods comprise `diff`, `re_match`, `lines_diff`, `re_match_multiline`, `contains`, and `sim_size`, all of which are either unsuitable for image data, or dangerous to be used (because the size of a file does not really represent its content). This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method, which is specifically tailored for single-channel and multi-channel image data (e.g., RGB), and can be used like this:

```xml
<test>
    <param name="in" value="actual.png" />
    <output name="out" value="expected.tif" compare="image_diff" metric="mae" />
</test>
```

The difference of a pair of images is quantified as the pixel-wise distance between the images, for which different *metrics* can be used. For different applications and different types of images (e.g., intensity vs. label images), different types of metrics are common. The following metrics are added for *intensity images:*

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for intensity images) |
| --- | --- |
| `mae` | Mean absolute error |
| `mse` | Mean squared error |
| `rms` | Root mean squared error |
| `fro` | Frobenius norm |

For label images (label maps), it is usually irrelevant which particular label is used for labeling an image region, but only the *sets of pixels* corresponding to individual image regions need to be compared. This can be accomplished using *one minus* the *intersection over  the union* of the regions:

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for label images) |
| --- | --- |
| `iou` | One minus the intersection over the union |

To decide whether a pair of images is equal, a tolerance is required, because different compression algorithms (or different versions thereof) can lead to different compression artifacts. A pair of images is considered to be *equal* in terms of the specified metric, if the distance of the images computed with respect to the metric is not above a threshold `eps` (0.01 by default).

:"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"As part of the Imaging Hackathon in Freiburg.

This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method for test output verification using images.

Previous comparison methods comprise `diff`, `re_match`, `lines_diff`, `re_match_multiline`, `contains`, and `sim_size`, all of which are either unsuitable for image data, or dangerous to be used (because the size of a file does not really represent its content). This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method, which is specifically tailored for single-channel and multi-channel image data (e.g., RGB), and can be used like this:

```xml
<test>
    <param name="in" value="actual.png" />
    <output name="out" value="expected.tif" compare="image_diff" metric="mae" />
</test>
```

The difference of a pair of images is quantified as the pixel-wise distance between the images, for which different *metrics* can be used. For different applications and different types of images (e.g., intensity vs. label images), different types of metrics are common. The following metrics are added for *intensity images:*

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for intensity images) |
| --- | --- |
| `mae` | Mean absolute error |
| `mse` | Mean squared error |
| `rms` | Root mean squared error |
| `fro` | Frobenius norm |

For label images (label maps), it is usually irrelevant which particular label is used for labeling an image region, but only the *sets of pixels* corresponding to individual image regions need to be compared. This can be accomplished using *one minus* the *intersection over  the union* of the regions:

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for label images) |
| --- | --- |
| `iou` | One minus the intersection over the union |

To decide whether a pair of images is equal, a tolerance is required, because different compression algorithms (or different versions thereof) can lead to different compression artifacts. A pair of images is considered to be *equal* in terms of the specified metric, if the distance of the images computed with respect to the metric is not above a threshold `eps` (0.01 by default).

:"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/17556.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"As part of the Imaging Hackathon in Freiburg.

This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method for test output verification using images.

Previous comparison methods comprise `diff`, `re_match`, `lines_diff`, `re_match_multiline`, `contains`, and `sim_size`, all of which are either unsuitable for image data, or dangerous to be used (because the size of a file does not really represent its content). This PR adds the `image_diff` comparison method, which is specifically tailored for single-channel and multi-channel image data (e.g., RGB), and can be used like this:

```xml
<test>
    <param name="in" value="actual.png" />
    <output name="out" value="expected.tif" compare="image_diff" metric="mae" />
</test>
```

The difference of a pair of images is quantified as the pixel-wise distance between the images, for which different *metrics* can be used. For different applications and different types of images (e.g., intensity vs. label images), different types of metrics are common. The following metrics are added for *intensity images:*

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for intensity images) |
| --- | --- |
| `mae` | Mean absolute error |
| `mse` | Mean squared error |
| `rms` | Root mean squared error |
| `fro` | Frobenius norm |

For label images (label maps), it is usually irrelevant which particular label is used for labeling an image region, but only the *sets of pixels* corresponding to individual image regions need to be compared. This can be accomplished using *one minus* the *intersection over  the union* of the regions:

| `metric` | Corresponding metric (for label images) |
| --- | --- |
| `iou` | One minus the intersection over the union |

To decide whether a pair of images is equal, a tolerance is required, because different compression algorithms (or different versions thereof) can lead to different compression artifacts. A pair of images is considered to be *equal* in terms of the specified metric, if the distance of the images computed with respect to the metric is not above a threshold `eps` (0.01 by default).

[GTN tutorial on viewing the site locally](https://training.galaxyproject.org/training-material/topics/contributing/tutorials/running-jekyll/tutorial.html) basically says that one should run
1. `make create-env`
2. `make install`

after installing Conda.

I have a fresh local clone of the repository. When running `make create-env` (step 1), an out of memory error occurs (error 137):

> (base) user@hostname:~/Documents/galaxy-training-material$ make create-env
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material/*/*/slides/*’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> if ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env list | grep '^galaxy_training_material'; then \
>     ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env update -f environment.yml; \
> else \
>     ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml; \
> fi
> Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
> Solving environment: \ bash: line 4: 3332886 Killed                  ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml
> make: *** [Makefile:43: create-env] Error 137

The same happens when I try running `make install` (step 2) after creating the environment manually using `mamba env create -f environment.yml`:

> (galaxy_training_material) user@hostname:~/Documents/galaxy-training-material$ make install
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material/*/*/slides/*’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> if ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env list | grep '^galaxy_training_material'; then \
>     ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env update -f environment.yml; \
> else \
>     ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml; \
> fi
> Retrieving notices: ...working... done
> Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
> Solving environment: \ bash: line 4: 3341561 Killed                  ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml

So how to install the requirements?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/galaxyproject/training-material/issues/4313/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/galaxyproject/training-material/issues/4313/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

the bug**
Boxplot fails to render. Tabular output is generated, but no graphic. Either in Galaxy or when downloaded ("blank png").
 
Seems related to https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/13437

**Galaxy Version and/or server at which you observed the bug**
Galaxy version 23.0.1.dev0, commit [6c4141bee122d505178160f9f1ce05a6563ef950](https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/commit/6c4141bee122d505178160f9f1ce05a6563ef950)
but also at UseGalaxy.eu.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a boxplot graphic
2. Wait for that to process
3. No graphic
4. Seems to loop back into creating the graphic if that is accessed from User > Visualizations

**Expected behavior**
Create a graphic. Other charts do plot on the same input, including the formatted output from this chart type.

**Screenshots**
If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem.

**Additional context**

History with output from the boxplot tool. Never renders.
https://usegalaxy.org/u/jen-galaxyproject/h/test-ghelp-8152

This is the blank graphic produced...
![New Chart](https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/assets/8999735/b2b7da34-bc35-4d87-88d9-37e2c7e3eff9)
... in between here.

-----
Xref https://help.galaxyproject.org/t/visualization-of-box-plot-jqplot-problem/8152/10

after installing Conda.

I have a fresh local clone of the repository. When running `make create-env` (step 1), an out of memory error occurs (error 137):

> (base) user@hostname:~/Documents/galaxy-training-material$ make create-env

the bug**
Boxplot fails to render. Tabular output is generated, but no graphic. Either in Galaxy or when downloaded ("blank png").
 
Seems related to https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/13437

**Galaxy Version and/or server at which you observed the bug**
Galaxy version 23.0.1.dev0, commit [6c4141bee122d505178160f9f1ce05a6563ef950](https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/commit/6c4141bee122d505178160f9f1ce05a6563ef950)
but also at UseGalaxy.eu.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a boxplot graphic
2. Wait for that to process
3. No graphic
4. Seems to loop back into creating the graphic if that is accessed from User > Visualizations

**Expected behavior**
Create a graphic. Other charts do plot on the same input, including the formatted output from this chart type.

**Screenshots**
If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem.

**Additional context**

History with output from the boxplot tool. Never renders.
https://usegalaxy.org/u/jen-galaxyproject/h/test-ghelp-8152

This is the blank graphic produced...
![New Chart](https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/assets/8999735/b2b7da34-bc35-4d87-88d9-37e2c7e3eff9)
... in between here.

-----
Xref https://help.galaxyproject.org/t/visualization-of-box-plot-jqplot-problem/8152/10

[GTN tutorial on viewing the site locally](https://training.galaxyproject.org/training-material/topics/contributing/tutorials/running-jekyll/tutorial.html) basically says that one should run
1. `make create-env`
2. `make install`

after installing Conda.

I have a fresh local clone of the repository. When running `make create-env` (step 1), an out of memory error occurs (error 137):

> (base) user@hostname:~/Documents/galaxy-training-material$ make create-env
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material/*/*/slides/*’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> if ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env list | grep '^galaxy_training_material'; then \
>     ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env update -f environment.yml; \
> else \
>     ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml; \
> fi
> Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
> Solving environment: \ bash: line 4: 3332886 Killed                  ~/.anaconda3/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml
> make: *** [Makefile:43: create-env] Error 137

The same happens when I try running `make install` (step 2) after creating the environment manually using `mamba env create -f environment.yml`:

> (galaxy_training_material) user@hostname:~/Documents/galaxy-training-material$ make install
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material/*/*/slides/*’: No such file or directory
> find: ‘_site/training-material’: No such file or directory
> if ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env list | grep '^galaxy_training_material'; then \
>     ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env update -f environment.yml; \
> else \
>     ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml; \
> fi
> Retrieving notices: ...working... done
> Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
> Solving environment: \ bash: line 4: 3341561 Killed                  ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/bin/conda env create -f environment.yml

Running `make install --dry-run` gave me the necessary hints to install the requirements manually:

1. `gem update --no-document --system`
2. `ICONV_LIBS="-L~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/lib/ -liconv" gem install --no-document addressable:'2.5.2' jekyll jekyll-feed jekyll-redirect-from csl-styles awesome_bot html-proofer pkg-config kwalify bibtex-ruby citeproc-ruby fastimage`
3. `pushd ~/.anaconda3/envs/galaxy_training_material/share/rubygems/bin`
4. `ln -sf ../../../bin/ruby ruby`

However, for some reason, running `make serve-quick` afterwards fails with the error
> EnvironmentNameNotFound: Could not find conda environment: galaxy_training_material
> You can list all discoverable environments with `conda info --envs`.

although I can see from `conda info --envs` that the environment is there.

So instead of using `make serve-quick`, one has to use:
```
mv Gemfile Gemfile.backup || true && \
mv Gemfile.lock Gemfile.lock.backup || true && \
jekyll serve --strict_front_matter -d _site/training-material --incremental --config _config.yml,_config-dev.yml -P 4000 -H 0.0.0.0 ""
```

However, I guess this is rather a workaround than a solution, so I'll leave this issue as open.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/galaxyproject/training-material/issues/comments/1671127918/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

I'm seeing the same error as the closed issue #18 (which was supposed to be fixed by #19) when trying to use the `imread` function to load an image:

```
error: imfinfo: support for Image IO was unavailable or disabled when Octave was built
error: called from
    __imread__ at line 87 column 10
    imageIO at line 141 column 28
    imread at line 102 column 33
```

Octave uses GraphicksMagic for this functionality and the package is installed in the conda list. I used micromamba to install, not sure how that could affect it. I also have the system Octave (V6.2) installed and there is no error (`imread` works fine).

This is on Debian 11 Raspberry Pi CM4.

### Installed packages

```shell

Bug","color":"e31c3d","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-10-18T13:07:22Z","updated_at":"2022-10-18T13:07:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description:

The documentation of `segmentation.watershed` says that:

> ### Returns
>   **out**: ndarray
>   A labeled matrix of the same type and shape as markers

[[0.18.x]](https://scikit-image.org/docs/0.18.x/api/skimage.segmentation.html#skimage.segmentation.watershed) [[0.19.x]](https://scikit-image.org/docs/0.19.x/api/skimage.segmentation.html#skimage.segmentation.watershed)

I have tested this with 0.18.1:

```python
import skimage.segmentation
import numpy as np
print(skimage.segmentation.watershed(np.zeros((100, 100)), np.zeros((100, 100), np.uint16)).dtype)
```

Gives `int32` but `uint16` is expected.

### Way to reproduce:

```Python
import skimage.segmentation
import numpy as np
print(skimage.segmentation.watershed(np.zeros((100, 100)), np.zeros((100, 100), np.uint16)).dtype)
```


### Traceback or output:

```Shell
int32
```


### Version information:

```Shell
3.8.5 (default, Sep  4 2020, 07:30:14) 
[GCC 7.3.0]
Linux-5.8.0-36-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.10
scikit-image version: 0.18.1
numpy version: 1.20.3
```
